content: Sleep disorders affect ~50 million Americans. Obstructive sleep apnea (OSA): repetitive upper airway collapse during sleep causing apneas/hypopneas, daytime sleepiness, cardiovascular risk. Risk factors: obesity, male sex, age, craniofacial abnormalities. Apnea-hypopnea index (AHI) severity: mild 5-15, moderate 15-30, severe >30 events/hour. Central sleep apnea: respiratory drive failure (heart failure, opioid use, stroke). Narcolepsy: chronic disorder of sleep-wake regulation with excessive daytime sleepiness plus cataplexy (type 1, orexin/hypocretin deficiency) or without cataplexy (type 2). Other features: sleep paralysis, hypnagogic hallucinations, disrupted nighttime sleep. Restless legs syndrome (RLS/Willis-Ekbom disease): urge to move legs with uncomfortable sensations, worse at rest/evening, relieved by movement; associated with iron deficiency, pregnancy, renal failure, neuropathy. Insomnia: difficulty initiating/maintaining sleep or non-restorative sleep ≥3 nights/week for ≥3 months with daytime impairment. Circadian rhythm disorders: delayed sleep phase, shift work disorder, jet lag.
